narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 33, full-time student status, and your low decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 grades—and estimated a 59.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure tipped the scales, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to succeed on the bar. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 grade decile, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 grade decile, ranked by their impact on the prediction. Chief among them, your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the typical student's 5.5—strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, signaling consistently low performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score of 33, while respectable, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, though not enough to offset other concerns, especially since the average is around 37. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed toward the failure prediction, reinforcing a pattern of underperformance compared to the average of about 5.7. Overall, your academic deciles stood out as much weaker than those of typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or higher, while your full-time status aligned with the vast majority (over 90%) and had little sway. This combination painted a picture of higher risk than the model deems acceptable for law school success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, strengthening your early and later undergraduate performance through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your potential despite these metrics. Many students in similar situations have improved their profiles and gained admission elsewhere—persistence and targeted improvements can change the narrative for your next application.
